item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: As the image has been confirmed to not comply with the SCP wiki's license (CC-BY-SA 3.0), I've removed the image.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for the feedback. I had planned to execute this better by extensively rewriting this… but Midterm Season just smacked me in the face and I joined a new research lab… so I currently have my work cut out for me :/

I really do not feel comfortable having something that can be vastly improved in mainlist wiki, but at same time, I can't prioritize editing this as of now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Overall, this was kind of compelling to follow, but overlong and had fairly major issues.

+ show block
– hide block

Things I liked: Their various methods for changing history, the Foundation morality question, some of the aesthetics, the kind of feeling of investigating a mystery I got throughout reading it.

My issues were primarily with clinical tone (the point where I downvoted was when you said anywhen in the description) and, probably more importantly, with text economy and overwrought grammar. You spend so long getting everything across, and sometimes it feels like you're making sentences complex to sound clinical or cram in extra detail (including things that aren't super relevant). There's a few essays (here and here) that might help with sentence structure, but paring back stuff is also important.

For actual content I had issues with, the tab about the ranks of crusaders felt generic and didn't add much. Them having a nearly 1 to 1 comparison to Foundation structure might have been foreshadowing, but it mostly felt like a lack of imagination. There were other sections that were similarly unhelpful or generic, such as the lecture about time and space. Repetition was also somewhat of an issue.

It's a little disappointing because that was an opportunity to give the GOI more character, which was otherwise lacking. For example, I get the impression that most of them wear union uniforms (which, by the way, need more context than "US Union" when first appearing) because their biggest operation was Gettysburg, so in all probability when you meet one of them, they were "just there." But what kind of people are they? When are they from? (If they're from the time of the actual crusades, or, really, any time but now, hoo boy, you should be ready for non-21st-century morality.)

Also, like… you don't have to explain everything (e.g., all of their protocols, what all of the O5 notes actually mean), especially when a lot of your SCP is a single expository note. Give us some credit as readers, let us figure some things out. Similarly, if you had to explain iron sheaf in the additional stapled pages to set up the plot twist that also occurs in those pages, that's not really foreshadowing/a twist. Edit: Like I think someone said above: Nazis win WWII? Not really a twist that demands explanation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed reading this, but the main theme of the article bothers me. I mean, if you have an organization dedicated to justice, and that organization has a time machine and weaponry from the future, why not just go back to the very first hunter-gatherer tribes and direct humanity from the beginning? You could instill concepts of justice, empathy, tolerance, and non-violent problem-solving generation after generation as humans spread across the globe. Why limit yourself to just making sure fewer people die in WWII, when you could stop the concept of war?

Of course, this may lead to a world were the Foundation doesn't exist.

item.title: Re: Thanks for Reading!
item.content:encoded:
A location link and the license.

Like this:

Image is "Lanius excubitor 1" by Marek Szczepanek. CC BY-SA 3.0. Downloaded from Wikimedia Commons.

item.title: Re: Thanks for Reading!
item.content:encoded:
Thank you so much for clarifying. I'll grab a relevant picture from Wikimedia commons and put it on the SCP file tomorrow… it is very late.

If I do get a picture from there, what should I put then for the aforementioned two categories?

item.title: Re: Thanks for Reading!
item.content:encoded:
Okay. I'll message the photographer on facebook as a last ditch effort and ask him if I can have permission to use it. If he doesn't answer, I'll remove it and replace it with a new one.

Permission isn't enough, I think we want to avoid any special exceptions like SCP-173. They would need to release it under CC BY SA 3.0 or a compatible license.

Another thing, how do you know which images are usable and won't cause copyright issues?

Any CC license that is less restrictive than or is BY SA, is good. Other copyleft schemes are sometimes compatible, but are much less common nowadays, when you do encounter one usually the license and CC will say if it is or is not compatible on their sites.

item.title: Re: Thanks for Reading!
item.content:encoded:
Darn it :(

Okay. I'll message the photographer on facebook as a last ditch effort and ask him if I can have permission to use it. If he doesn't answer, I'll remove it and replace it with a new one.

Another thing, how do you know which images are usable and won't cause copyright issues?

item.title: Re: Thanks for Reading!
item.content:encoded:
but I am not sure what I should put for the 'picture location' and the 'license'.

Since it appears to be copyright you don't apparently have a license to use it and you should just remove it. :(

Unless you do have proof that it is available under a compliant license in which case you should say what license and link to your proof.

item.title: Re: Thanks for Reading!
item.content:encoded: Okay. I originally found this picture on Reddit, but after some google reverse image searching, the original owner of the photo seems to be Birinyi Krisztian, a photographer who displays his photographs Here. I have linked the site of the photograph's owner, but I am not sure what I should put for the 'picture location' and the 'license'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I googled what a TARDIS was and I just spent an hour going down the Dr. Who rabbit hole. Damn it Mark why did you have to do this to me on a weekday :(

In all seriousness, thank you for the suggestions and the compliments. As far as your headcanon goes, it is definitely possible, so I wouldn't call it irrational at all.

I know that in the list of 'Missions' that the Silent Crusade undertook, the one for the Amisitar Massacre is the only one that deviates from IRL history. I originally intended it to be a mix of IRL history getting better, or IRL history being the 'better' version that was improved upon, but I somehow ended up with only 1 of the former. But yeah, if reading that Wikipedia article made you sad, don't watch the movie Gandhi :/ there's a scene there that features this exact event.

And on the technical side of things, how would I fix the issue you described regarding the picture? The original picture was rather large and had parts I did not want, so I used the snipping tool to trim out the part I wanted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for your suggestions and compliments. I will look for missing periods. Capitalization and other punctuations, however, are a bit hard for me because English is not my first language. I'll consult a source where the grammatical rules for those can be found… possibly over the weekend when school isn't deluging me with work. If you are willing, could you point out some of the glaring errors so I can fix them ASAP?

The whole overwrought language thing was a remnant of me trying to make the Silent Crusade out to be some kind of a fanatical organization, but the emphasis of the SCP fell more on the internal schism/ethical dilemma that the Foundation now face. Maybe in the interview log, I can have one of the men speak 'normally' to show that The Silent Crusade picked up members from many different time periods?

I do admit that I was being very verbose in this SCP, but at this point, I recognize the fact that I am not an experienced enough writer to know what exactly I should condense. I am afraid of taking out something that may have been crucial to the story. Do you mind if I do some condensing in sandbox and present it to you for feedback?

Yeah. I think I can take out Protocol Sleeping Nephilim and I can still keep the essence of the Foundation making some other excuses for when SCP-4403-1 going missing is inevitably found out.

item.title: Re: Thanks for Reading!
item.content:encoded: Typically it's picture location, picture creator/owner, and the license it's released under.

item.title: Re: Thanks for Reading!
item.content:encoded: How do I attribute the picture? Do I just list where I got the picture from in the discussion section?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bad:
+ show block
– hide block
  • Literally a TARDIS. Like, I don't even want to start listing the ways that it's a Dr Who canon TARDIS just with the serial numbers filed off and some of the adjectives changed.
  • Under the command of cartoonish white-knighting Timelords traversing time to make history and the future much less shit and keep humanity safe.
  • Foundation has intervened essentially to… er… ensure things stay shit? Maybe worried that if all the bad things in the world are done away with they won't have any reason to exist?
  • No picture attribution, and the hosted version is weirdly low rez but has at least twice as many physical pixels as it needs? (Like someone opened it, did a nearest-neighbour upscale, and resaved it)

Neutral:

  • Not sure what's going on with the Amritsar Massacre entry, as unlike all the others it doesn't actually align with baseline reality (the "pre-intervention" version is what happened IRL), and clicking through and reading the Wikipedia article made me do a sad :( … I feel like there's supposed to be some kind of backstory or deeper implication there but I'm not getting it. May just be me though.
Good:
+ show block
– hide block
  • I really like the picture. Like I want it as a wallpaper. Very nice.
  • It's not… the worst idea ever. And we are looking for worn-out cliches after all.
  • The recovered documents thing really saves it, and it's clear you've done a decent bit of history research, or at least know your stuff in general. And the WP links are interesting if you don't already know the details of the event listed.
  • Ditto the "stapled note" expansion, which is really the meat of the whole thing and raises a few moral questions, as well as exactly what O5-1 is playing at and why…

Out of interest, on a scale of 173 to 682, how irrational is my headcanon that the throng of rebelling Foundation staff are actually, ultimately, the group that set up the Silent Crusade (a meaningful name for a hidden faction within another organisation with self-proclaimed higher morals than the rest of it) as a reaction to these events? Or at least end up causing some kind of Stable Time Loop, taking over and re-staffing the Tardis Train (seeing as much of the previous crew would have been killed in the Foundation raid, and who knows how many years they've been doing it according to the machine's internal timeframe?), and ultimately coming back to (maybe a different, altered timeline version of) the Foundation's attention much further down the, ahem, line…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm on the fence:

+ show block
– hide block
  • This isn't the first time machine I've seen the Foundation use — including a personal favorite. There's nothing novel about this variant.
  • There's numerous (minor, but still significant) errors in regards to punctuation (missing periods), capitalization, and grammar.
  • There's lots of parts where the article could be significantly trimmed without losing a thing.
  • There's a lot of moving parts here that I don't think are necessary (like the whole 'Protocol Nephilim').
  • I'm kind of 'meh' about the Silent Crusade's overwrought language (and even the description of their internal structure, which I think is probably not necessary here).
  • However: I really like the idea of the Foundation struggling with the moral weight of having a time-machine that works, and proof (via the Silent Crusade) that it's already been used to make the world a better place.
  • I also really like the mechanic of an addendum that's just a sheathe of papers that anyone could take off, and the proof that the author is not alone in their thinking caused by the sheathe still being there.
  • Plus the angle of Old Guard versus new Guard.
  • Finally, I like the implication (possibly unintentional, here) that the Silent Crusade might, in fact, be the Foundation defectors themselves — just displaced in time.

All this adds up to a no-vote: I like the angle you're going for, but I think the execution could use significant tightening. I might come back to this later and give it a second read-through to see if I feel more strongly.

item.title: Re: Thanks for Reading!
item.content:encoded: Picture is really awesome, but needs to be attributed.

item.title: Re: Thanks for Reading!
item.content:encoded:
I apologize for the grammatical errors. English is not my first language. I am not sure if they are simple ones or more complex ones like that involving sentence structure, but I will skim through the SCP and attempt to make fixes. Thank you for pointing that out.

I did not plan on having a humorous effect with the footnotes, and if you could point out in specific which could be construed as such, that would also be appreciated.

You do make a very valid point in that retroactively removing people from the timeline is a significant aftereffect.

It was definitely something that came up while I was working on this SCP, but ultimately, I thought that if utilitarianism was how they quantified how much good they were doing, then exchange of lives would be deemed acceptable by the newer generation.

I know this is a cheesy analogy but if you asked people if they had the power to go back in time and prevent a busfull of people from dying in an accident, chances are a good amount of people would say yes, despite the potential that their actions may ripple out and 'erase' people that would have existed in the future.

As far as the 'Theme' goes, I am not sure what you mean by theme.

Regardless, Thank you very much for the feedback you have provided. I am now a better writer because of it.

item.title: Re: Thanks for Reading!
item.content:encoded: I'll fix that. Thank you for pointing that out!

item.title: Re: Thanks for Reading!
item.content:encoded: There are few SCP-XXXXs at the final collapsible.

item.title: Re: Thanks for Reading!
item.content:encoded: You have numerous grammatical errors that you could fix, and a lot of the footnotes and the motions read as lolFoundation. Not a huge fan of alternate timeline super-Nazis who only lost because of Hitler, either. And just personally, I don't like how this "idealistic younger generation" is casually committing atrocities we can barely even imagine (retroactively removing people from existence). I don't like the theme of your SCP.

item.title: Re: Thanks for Reading!
item.content:encoded:
That's… weird. This is the first time I've posted this SCP in the mainlist page. I did work on this for about 2~3 weeks on the sandbox page, however.

Here is the link to the sandbox page as proof. If you click on history, it should show the dates of edits.

http://scp-sandbox-3.wikidot.com/cliche-contest-entry

I sincerely hope that it was some similar SCP and not actually someone copy-pasting off the sandbox page.

It also seems like not many people really liked my SCP. I would love to know how to improve, so feedback would be awesome.

item.title: Re: Thanks for Reading!
item.content:encoded: I feel like I've seen this before. Wasn't this posted some time ago and deleted?

item.title: Thanks for Reading!
item.content:encoded:
Update 8/11/19 Completed 1st draft of the Rewrite, Getting feedback.

Update 6/18/19: Rewrite started.

Update 1/23/19: So after receiving a lot of feedback, I feel like this SCP can be made better. However, extensive edits are against contest rules, and I do not want to risk altering something that could be seen as fundamental. Therefore, I'll return to making this better after the contest ends, as to not violate the rules.

Cliche: #4: A doorway of any shape to a different dimension/timeline/world

Story Element Cliches: #3 (GoI/PoI here) gets one over the Foundation, making them look like fools in the process.

Thank you to all the kind people on #site19 and #thecritters that gave me encouragement and feedback :)

IF YOU MISSED IT, BE SURE TO HIGHLIGHT THE BOTTOM OF THE PAGE AND CLICK ON THE COLLAPSIBLE

The Picture was taken by Krisztian Birinyi Here.

If you think about it, there's been snippets here and there that suggest people like the O5 council live for a fantastically long amount of time thanks to anomalous means of life extension.

Some of these people have been around since the days when there have been virtually no research done on many anomalies or when there were no Scranton reality anchors to protect them from the wrath of seemingly unstoppable reality benders. It is understandable why they would be so careful and reluctant to utilize a lot of these anomalous items.

On the other hand, the newer generation has access to all the data and research accumulated over the years, and they have a blank slate, a fresh point of view to put two and two together.

Aside from the cliches, I wanted to write an SCP that showed how the inevitable gap between generations, between the 'old guard' that had been there when the Foundation was in its nascent years, to the more idealistic newer generation would lead to a conflict.

Thanks for Reading! If you have any feedback/questions, please leave them here, and I will try to address them ASAP!
